retrieved_sections.title: ACCELERATION
retrieved_sections.summary: This section defines acceleration as the rate of change of velocity with time, distinguishing between average and instantaneous acceleration. It explains how to interpret acceleration graphically using position-time and velocity-time graphs, and demonstrates that the area under a velocity-time graph represents displacement.
retrieved_sections.subject: physics

retrieved_sections.title: VELOCITY AND ACCELERATION IN SIMPLE HARMONIC MOTION
retrieved_sections.summary: This section derives and explains the expressions for velocity and acceleration in simple harmonic motion (SHM) using both the geometric reference circle method and calculus. It also details the phase relationships among displacement, velocity, and acceleration, illustrating them with synchronized plots and numerical calculations.
retrieved_sections.subject: physics

retrieved_sections.title: MOTION IN A PLANE WITH CONSTANT ACCELERATION
retrieved_sections.summary: This section derives the vector equations of motion for an object moving in a plane with constant acceleration. It establishes that two-dimensional motion can be resolved into two independent, simultaneous one-dimensional motions along perpendicular axes.
retrieved_sections.subject: physics

retrieved_sections.title: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
retrieved_sections.summary: This section explains how to calculate the gravitational force exerted by the Earth on objects located both outside and inside its volume using the shell theorem. It derives the formula for the acceleration due to gravity (g) at the Earth's surface and demonstrates how this value can be used to estimate the Earth's mass.
retrieved_sections.subject: physics

retrieved_sections.title: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
retrieved_sections.summary: This section derives and analyzes the variation of acceleration due to gravity both above and below the Earth's surface. It demonstrates that gravity is at its maximum on the surface and decreases with both altitude and depth.
retrieved_sections.subject: physics

retrieved_sections.title: UNIFORM CIRCULAR MOTION
retrieved_sections.summary: This section introduces uniform circular motion, defining it as motion along a circular path at a constant speed. It derives the direction and magnitude of centripetal acceleration and establishes the relationships between linear speed, angular speed, time period, and frequency.
retrieved_sections.subject: physics

retrieved_sections.title: THE LAW OF INERTIA
retrieved_sections.summary: This section explores Galileo's experiments with inclined and double inclined planes, which challenged the Aristotelian view of motion. These observations led to the definition of inertia as a body's natural resistance to changing its state of rest or uniform motion.
retrieved_sections.subject: physics

retrieved_sections.title: PROJECTILE MOTION
retrieved_sections.summary: This section introduces projectile motion as the combination of independent horizontal motion with constant velocity and vertical motion with constant acceleration due to gravity. It derives the parabolic trajectory equation and formulas for key parameters including time of flight, maximum height, and horizontal range.
retrieved_sections.subject: physics

prerequisite_questions.label: Derivative
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Derivative?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: Instantaneous acceleration is defined mathematically as the derivative of velocity with respect to time.

prerequisite_questions.label: Slope of a Line
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Slope of a Line?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: Understanding how to find the slope of a line and a tangent is necessary to interpret acceleration from velocity-time graphs.

prerequisite_questions.label: Velocity
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Velocity?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity, so the learner must understand velocity and its instantaneous and average forms.

prerequisite_questions.label: Differentiation
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Differentiation?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: Calculus-based derivations of velocity and acceleration require differentiating displacement and velocity functions with respect to time.

prerequisite_questions.label: Displacement in Simple Harmonic Motion
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Displacement in Simple Harmonic Motion?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: The derivations of velocity and acceleration require the mathematical expression for displacement in SHM as a starting point.

prerequisite_questions.label: Uniform Circular Motion
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Uniform Circular Motion?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: The reference circle method relies on projecting the tangential velocity and centripetal acceleration of uniform circular motion onto a diameter.

prerequisite_questions.label: Constant Acceleration in One Dimension
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Constant Acceleration in One Dimension?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: The derivation and understanding of 2D equations of motion build directly upon the 1D equations of motion with constant acceleration.

prerequisite_questions.label: Vector Components
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Vector Components?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: Splitting vector equations into independent x and y scalar components requires prior knowledge of vector resolution.

prerequisite_questions.label: Density
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Density?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: The concept of uniform density is necessary to calculate the mass of the Earth's inner concentric spheres as a function of radius.

prerequisite_questions.label: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: The derivations of gravitational forces inside and outside the Earth rely directly on the mathematical formulation of Newton's law of gravitation.

prerequisite_questions.label: Newton's Second Law of Motion
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Newton's Second Law of Motion?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: Newton's second law (F = ma) is required to relate the gravitational force on a mass to its acceleration due to gravity (g).

prerequisite_questions.label: Binomial Approximation
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Binomial Approximation?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: Used to simplify the expression for gravity at small heights relative to the Earth's radius.

prerequisite_questions.label: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: The derivations of gravitational force at a height and depth rely directly on the inverse-square law of gravitation.

prerequisite_questions.label: Shell Theorem
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Shell Theorem?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: The derivation of gravity at a depth assumes that the outer spherical shell of Earth exerts no net gravitational force on an interior mass.

prerequisite_questions.label: Instantaneous Acceleration

prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: Centripetal acceleration is defined as the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero.

prerequisite_questions.label: Position and Velocity Vectors
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Position and Velocity Vectors?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: The derivation of circular motion kinematics requires representing position and velocity as vectors that change direction over time.

prerequisite_questions.label: Vector Subtraction
prerequisite_questions.question: How comfortable are you with Vector Subtraction?
prerequisite_questions.pedagogical_reason: Finding the direction and magnitude of the change in velocity vector (\Delta \mathbf{v}) relies on vector subtraction and the triangle law of vector addition.

main_path_sections.title: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
main_path_sections.summary: This section explains how to calculate the gravitational force exerted by the Earth on objects located both outside and inside its volume using the shell theorem. It derives the formula for the acceleration due to gravity (g) at the Earth's surface and demonstrates how this value can be used to estimate the Earth's mass.

teaches.label: Acceleration due to Gravity
teaches.teaching_evidence: we obtain the standard formula for acceleration due to gravity at the surface: g = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{GM_E}{R_E^2}.
teaches.evidence_text: we obtain the standard formula for acceleration due to gravity at the surface: g = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{GM_E}{R_E^2}.

teaches.label: Estimation of Earth's Mass
teaches.teaching_evidence: enables one to estimate the mass of the earth M_E from the relation M_E = \frac{g R_E^2}{G}.
teaches.evidence_text: enables one to estimate the mass of the earth M_E from the relation M_E = \frac{g R_E^2}{G}.

teaches.label: Gravitational Force Inside the Earth
teaches.teaching_evidence: inside the earth, the gravitational force is directly proportional to the distance r from the centre.
teaches.evidence_text: inside the earth, the gravitational force is directly proportional to the distance r from the centre.

teaches.label: Shell Theorem for Gravitation
teaches.teaching_evidence: According to the shell theorem, for a point mass outside the Earth, each shell exerts a force as if its mass were concentrated at the center.
teaches.evidence_text: According to the shell theorem, for a point mass outside the Earth, each shell exerts a force as if its mass were concentrated at the center.

requires.evidence_text: The derivations of gravitational forces inside and outside the Earth rely directly on the mathematical formulation of Newton's law of gravitation.

requires.label: Newton's Second Law of Motion
requires.pedagogical_reason: Newton's second law (F = ma) is required to relate the gravitational force on a mass to its acceleration due to gravity (g).
requires.evidence_text: Newton's second law (F = ma) is required to relate the gravitational force on a mass to its acceleration due to gravity (g).

main_path_sections.title: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
main_path_sections.summary: This section derives and analyzes the variation of acceleration due to gravity both above and below the Earth's surface. It demonstrates that gravity is at its maximum on the surface and decreases with both altitude and depth.

teaches.label: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Depth
teaches.teaching_evidence: The acceleration due to gravity at depth d is g(d) = g(1 - d/R_E).
teaches.evidence_text: The acceleration due to gravity at depth d is g(d) = g(1 - d/R_E).
source_labels: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Depth

teaches.label: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Height
teaches.teaching_evidence: The acceleration experienced by the point mass is g(h) = GM_E / (R_E + h)^2.
teaches.evidence_text: The acceleration experienced by the point mass is g(h) = GM_E / (R_E + h)^2.
source_labels: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Height

teaches.label: Approximation of Gravity for Small Heights
teaches.teaching_evidence: Using the binomial expression for h/R_E << 1, we get the approximation: g(h) = g(1 - 2h/R_E).
teaches.evidence_text: Using the binomial expression for h/R_E << 1, we get the approximation: g(h) = g(1 - 2h/R_E).
source_labels: Approximation of Gravity for Small Heights

teaches.label: Variation of Gravity with Position
teaches.teaching_evidence: The acceleration due to earth's gravity is maximum on its surface, decreasing whether you go up... or down.
teaches.evidence_text: The acceleration due to earth's gravity is maximum on its surface, decreasing whether you go up... or down.

teaches.teaching_evidence: enables one to estimate the mass of the earth M_E from the relation M_E = \frac{g R_E^2}{G}.
teaches.evidence_text: enables one to estimate the mass of the earth M_E from the relation M_E = \frac{g R_E^2}{G}.

teaches.label: Gravitational Force Inside the Earth
teaches.teaching_evidence: inside the earth, the gravitational force is directly proportional to the distance r from the centre.
teaches.evidence_text: inside the earth, the gravitational force is directly proportional to the distance r from the centre.

teaches.label: Shell Theorem for Gravitation
teaches.teaching_evidence: According to the shell theorem, for a point mass outside the Earth, each shell exerts a force as if its mass were concentrated at the center.
teaches.evidence_text: According to the shell theorem, for a point mass outside the Earth, each shell exerts a force as if its mass were concentrated at the center.

requires.label: Density
requires.pedagogical_reason: The concept of uniform density is necessary to calculate the mass of the Earth's inner concentric spheres as a function of radius.
requires.evidence_text: The concept of uniform density is necessary to calculate the mass of the Earth's inner concentric spheres as a function of radius.

requires.label: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
requires.pedagogical_reason: The derivations of gravitational forces inside and outside the Earth rely directly on the mathematical formulation of Newton's law of gravitation.
requires.evidence_text: The derivations of gravitational forces inside and outside the Earth rely directly on the mathematical formulation of Newton's law of gravitation.

requires.label: Newton's Second Law of Motion
requires.pedagogical_reason: Newton's second law (F = ma) is required to relate the gravitational force on a mass to its acceleration due to gravity (g).
requires.evidence_text: Newton's second law (F = ma) is required to relate the gravitational force on a mass to its acceleration due to gravity (g).

target_sections.title: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
target_sections.summary: This section derives and analyzes the variation of acceleration due to gravity both above and below the Earth's surface. It demonstrates that gravity is at its maximum on the surface and decreases with both altitude and depth.

teaches.label: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Depth
teaches.teaching_evidence: The acceleration due to gravity at depth d is g(d) = g(1 - d/R_E).
teaches.evidence_text: The acceleration due to gravity at depth d is g(d) = g(1 - d/R_E).
source_labels: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Depth

teaches.label: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Height
teaches.teaching_evidence: The acceleration experienced by the point mass is g(h) = GM_E / (R_E + h)^2.
teaches.evidence_text: The acceleration experienced by the point mass is g(h) = GM_E / (R_E + h)^2.
source_labels: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Height

teaches.label: Approximation of Gravity for Small Heights
teaches.teaching_evidence: Using the binomial expression for h/R_E << 1, we get the approximation: g(h) = g(1 - 2h/R_E).
teaches.evidence_text: Using the binomial expression for h/R_E << 1, we get the approximation: g(h) = g(1 - 2h/R_E).
source_labels: Approximation of Gravity for Small Heights

teaches.label: Variation of Gravity with Position
teaches.teaching_evidence: The acceleration due to earth's gravity is maximum on its surface, decreasing whether you go up... or down.
teaches.evidence_text: The acceleration due to earth's gravity is maximum on its surface, decreasing whether you go up... or down.

requires.label: Binomial Approximation
requires.pedagogical_reason: Used to simplify the expression for gravity at small heights relative to the Earth's radius.
requires.evidence_text: Used to simplify the expression for gravity at small heights relative to the Earth's radius.

requires.label: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
requires.pedagogical_reason: The derivations of gravitational force at a height and depth rely directly on the inverse-square law of gravitation.
requires.evidence_text: The derivations of gravitational force at a height and depth rely directly on the inverse-square law of gravitation.

requires.label: Shell Theorem
requires.pedagogical_reason: The derivation of gravity at a depth assumes that the outer spherical shell of Earth exerts no net gravitational force on an interior mass.
requires.evidence_text: The derivation of gravity at a depth assumes that the outer spherical shell of Earth exerts no net gravitational force on an interior mass.

support_sections.title: UNIFORM CIRCULAR MOTION
support_sections.summary: This section introduces uniform circular motion, defining it as motion along a circular path at a constant speed. It derives the direction and magnitude of centripetal acceleration and establishes the relationships between linear speed, angular speed, time period, and frequency.

teaches.label: Angular Displacement and Angular Speed
teaches.teaching_evidence: \Delta \theta is called angular displacement. We define the angular speed \omega ... as the time rate of change of angular displacement
teaches.evidence_text: \Delta \theta is called angular displacement. We define the angular speed \omega ... as the time rate of change of angular displacement

teaches.label: Centripetal Acceleration
teaches.teaching_evidence: the centripetal acceleration a_c is : a_c = v^2/R
teaches.evidence_text: the centripetal acceleration a_c is : a_c = v^2/R

teaches.label: Relation between Linear Speed and Angular Speed
teaches.teaching_evidence: Thus, the relation between linear speed v and angular speed \omega is: v = R \omega
teaches.evidence_text: Thus, the relation between linear speed v and angular speed \omega is: v = R \omega
source_labels: Relation between Linear Speed and Angular Speed

teaches.label: Time Period and Frequency in Circular Motion
teaches.teaching_evidence: The time taken by an object to make one revolution is known as its time period T.
teaches.evidence_text: The time taken by an object to make one revolution is known as its time period T.
source_labels: Time Period and Frequency in Circular Motion

teaches.label: Uniform Circular Motion
teaches.teaching_evidence: When an object follows a circular path at a constant speed, the motion of the object is called uniform circular motion.
teaches.evidence_text: When an object follows a circular path at a constant speed, the motion of the object is called uniform circular motion.

requires.label: Instantaneous Acceleration
requires.pedagogical_reason: Centripetal acceleration is defined as the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero.
requires.evidence_text: Centripetal acceleration is defined as the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero.

requires.label: Position and Velocity Vectors
requires.pedagogical_reason: The derivation of circular motion kinematics requires representing position and velocity as vectors that change direction over time.
requires.evidence_text: The derivation of circular motion kinematics requires representing position and velocity as vectors that change direction over time.

requires.label: Vector Subtraction
requires.pedagogical_reason: Finding the direction and magnitude of the change in velocity vector (\Delta \mathbf{v}) relies on vector subtraction and the triangle law of vector addition.
requires.evidence_text: Finding the direction and magnitude of the change in velocity vector (\Delta \mathbf{v}) relies on vector subtraction and the triangle law of vector addition.

support_sections.title: THE LAW OF INERTIA
support_sections.summary: This section explores Galileo's experiments with inclined and double inclined planes, which challenged the Aristotelian view of motion. These observations led to the definition of inertia as a body's natural resistance to changing its state of rest or uniform motion.

teaches.label: Galileo's Double Inclined Plane Experiment
teaches.teaching_evidence: In the limiting case where the slope of the second plane is zero (horizontal), the ball travels an infinite distance, meaning its motion never ceases
teaches.evidence_text: In the limiting case where the slope of the second plane is zero (horizontal), the ball travels an infinite distance, meaning its motion never ceases

teaches.label: Galileo's Inclined Plane Observations
teaches.teaching_evidence: objects moving down an inclined plane accelerate, (ii) objects moving up an inclined plane retard, and (iii) motion on a horizontal plane is an intermediate situation.
teaches.evidence_text: objects moving down an inclined plane accelerate, (ii) objects moving up an inclined plane retard, and (iii) motion on a horizontal plane is an intermediate situation.

teaches.label: Inertia
teaches.teaching_evidence: The property of a body to resist change in its state of rest or uniform motion is called inertia.
teaches.evidence_text: The property of a body to resist change in its state of rest or uniform motion is called inertia.

requires.label: Acceleration
requires.pedagogical_reason: The student must understand acceleration and retardation to follow Galileo's observations of motion on inclined planes.
requires.evidence_text: The student must understand acceleration and retardation to follow Galileo's observations of motion on inclined planes.

requires.label: Force
requires.pedagogical_reason: The concept of inertia is defined in relation to the presence or absence of a net external force.
requires.evidence_text: The concept of inertia is defined in relation to the presence or absence of a net external force.

requires.label: Velocity
requires.pedagogical_reason: Understanding constant velocity is necessary to comprehend Galileo's conclusions about motion on horizontal planes.
requires.evidence_text: Understanding constant velocity is necessary to comprehend Galileo's conclusions about motion on horizontal planes.

support_sections.title: PROJECTILE MOTION
support_sections.summary: This section introduces projectile motion as the combination of independent horizontal motion with constant velocity and vertical motion with constant acceleration due to gravity. It derives the parabolic trajectory equation and formulas for key parameters including time of flight, maximum height, and horizontal range.

teaches.label: Horizontal Range of a Projectile
teaches.teaching_evidence: The horizontal distance travelled by a projectile from its initial position to the position where it passes y=0 during its fall is called the horizontal range, R.
teaches.evidence_text: The horizontal distance travelled by a projectile from its initial position to the position where it passes y=0 during its fall is called the horizontal range, R.

teaches.label: Maximum Height of a Projectile
teaches.teaching_evidence: The maximum height hm reached by the projectile can be calculated by substituting t = tm
teaches.evidence_text: The maximum height hm reached by the projectile can be calculated by substituting t = tm

teaches.label: Projectile Motion
teaches.teaching_evidence: A projectile is an object in flight after being thrown or projected... Projectile motion is the result of two independent, simultaneous components
teaches.evidence_text: A projectile is an object in flight after being thrown or projected... Projectile motion is the result of two independent, simultaneous components

teaches.label: Time of Flight
teaches.teaching_evidence: The total time Tf during which the projectile is in flight... Tf is known as the time of flight of the projectile.
teaches.evidence_text: The total time Tf during which the projectile is in flight... Tf is known as the time of flight of the projectile.

teaches.label: Trajectory of a Projectile
teaches.teaching_evidence: This is the equation of a parabola, i.e. the path of the projectile is a parabola
teaches.evidence_text: This is the equation of a parabola, i.e. the path of the projectile is a parabola

requires.label: Acceleration due to Gravity
requires.pedagogical_reason: The vertical component of projectile motion is modeled as free fall under constant gravitational acceleration.
requires.evidence_text: The vertical component of projectile motion is modeled as free fall under constant gravitational acceleration.

requires.label: Equations of Motion
requires.pedagogical_reason: Analyzing projectile motion requires applying kinematic equations of motion independently to the horizontal and vertical directions.
requires.evidence_text: Analyzing projectile motion requires applying kinematic equations of motion independently to the horizontal and vertical directions.

requires.label: Vector Resolution
requires.pedagogical_reason: The initial velocity vector must be resolved into horizontal and vertical components using trigonometric functions to set up the equations of motion.
requires.evidence_text: The initial velocity vector must be resolved into horizontal and vertical components using trigonometric functions to set up the equations of motion.

support_sections.title: Differentiation, Dedifferentiation and Redifferentiation
support_sections.summary: This section explains the developmental processes of differentiation, dedifferentiation, and redifferentiation in plant cells. It describes how cells mature, regain division capacity, and mature again, highlighting that plant differentiation is an open process determined by cell location.

teaches.label: Dedifferentiation
teaches.teaching_evidence: living differentiated cells to regain the capacity to divide under specific conditions, a phenomenon known as dedifferentiation.
teaches.evidence_text: living differentiated cells to regain the capacity to divide under specific conditions, a phenomenon known as dedifferentiation.

teaches.label: Differentiation
teaches.teaching_evidence: Cells derived from root apical meristems... undergo maturation to perform specific functions, a process called differentiation.
teaches.evidence_text: Cells derived from root apical meristems... undergo maturation to perform specific functions, a process called differentiation.

teaches.label: Open Differentiation
teaches.teaching_evidence: differentiation in plants is considered 'open' because the final structure of a cell or tissue at maturity is often determined by its location
teaches.evidence_text: differentiation in plants is considered 'open' because the final structure of a cell or tissue at maturity is often determined by its location

teaches.label: Redifferentiation
teaches.teaching_evidence: new cells eventually lose their capacity to divide again... this subsequent maturation is called redifferentiation.
teaches.evidence_text: new cells eventually lose their capacity to divide again... this subsequent maturation is called redifferentiation.

requires.label: Meristems
requires.pedagogical_reason: The section assumes prior knowledge of root apical meristems, shoot apical meristems, and cambium as the source tissues that undergo differentiation.
requires.evidence_text: The section assumes prior knowledge of root apical meristems, shoot apical meristems, and cambium as the source tissues that undergo differentiation.

requires.label: Parenchyma
requires.pedagogical_reason: The text mentions parenchyma cells as the starting point for dedifferentiation into meristems like interfascicular cambium.
requires.evidence_text: The text mentions parenchyma cells as the starting point for dedifferentiation into meristems like interfascicular cambium.

support_sections.title: PROPERTIES OF MATTER AND THEIR MEASUREMENT
support_sections.summary: This section introduces the classification of matter's properties into physical and chemical categories and details the International System of Units (SI) used for quantitative measurements. It defines the seven fundamental base units, SI prefixes, and explains key physical quantities including mass, weight, volume, density, and temperature scales.

teaches.label: Density
teaches.teaching_evidence: Density is a physical property that relates the mass of a substance to its volume. It is defined as the amount of mass per unit volume
teaches.evidence_text: Density is a physical property that relates the mass of a substance to its volume. It is defined as the amount of mass per unit volume

teaches.label: International System of Units (SI)
teaches.teaching_evidence: The SI system has seven base units and they are listed in Table 1.1. These units pertain to the seven fundamental scientific quantities.
teaches.evidence_text: The SI system has seven base units and they are listed in Table 1.1. These units pertain to the seven fundamental scientific quantities.

teaches.label: Mass and Weight
teaches.teaching_evidence: Mass of a substance is the amount of matter present in it, while weight is the force exerted by gravity on an object.
teaches.evidence_text: Mass of a substance is the amount of matter present in it, while weight is the force exerted by gravity on an object.

teaches.label: Physical and Chemical Properties
teaches.teaching_evidence: Physical Properties: These can be measured or observed without changing the identity... Chemical Properties: ...requires a chemical change
teaches.evidence_text: Physical Properties: These can be measured or observed without changing the identity... Chemical Properties: ...requires a chemical change

teaches.label: Temperature Scales
teaches.teaching_evidence: Temperature is commonly measured using three scales: Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin. Kelvin is the SI unit of temperature.
teaches.evidence_text: Temperature is commonly measured using three scales: Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin. Kelvin is the SI unit of temperature.

requires.label: Basic Algebraic Equations
requires.pedagogical_reason: Required to perform unit conversions, calculate density, and convert temperatures between Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin scales.
requires.evidence_text: Required to perform unit conversions, calculate density, and convert temperatures between Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin scales.

requires.label: Matter
requires.pedagogical_reason: The student must understand what matter is before studying its physical and chemical properties, mass, volume, and density.
requires.evidence_text: The student must understand what matter is before studying its physical and chemical properties, mass, volume, and density.

support_sections.title: Pressure
support_sections.summary: This section introduces the concept of pressure as the normal force exerted per unit area, explaining its scalar nature, SI units, and behavior in static fluids. It also defines fluid density and relative density, illustrating these concepts with physical examples and data.

teaches.label: Density
teaches.teaching_evidence: Density rho for a fluid of mass m occupying volume V is defined as: rho = m/V
teaches.evidence_text: Density rho for a fluid of mass m occupying volume V is defined as: rho = m/V

teaches.label: Normal Force in Fluids
teaches.teaching_evidence: When an object is submerged in a fluid at rest, the fluid exerts a force on its surface that is always normal (perpendicular) to the surface
teaches.evidence_text: When an object is submerged in a fluid at rest, the fluid exerts a force on its surface that is always normal (perpendicular) to the surface

teaches.label: Pascal
teaches.teaching_evidence: its SI unit is the pascal (Pa), where 1 Pa = 1 N m^-2
teaches.evidence_text: its SI unit is the pascal (Pa), where 1 Pa = 1 N m^-2

teaches.label: Pressure
teaches.teaching_evidence: average pressure P_av is defined as the normal force acting per unit area: P_av = F/A
teaches.evidence_text: average pressure P_av is defined as the normal force acting per unit area: P_av = F/A

teaches.label: Relative Density
teaches.teaching_evidence: Relative density is the dimensionless ratio of a substance's density to the density of water at 4 degrees Celsius
teaches.evidence_text: Relative density is the dimensionless ratio of a substance's density to the density of water at 4 degrees Celsius

requires.label: Force
requires.pedagogical_reason: Pressure is defined mathematically as force per unit area, requiring a prior understanding of force.
requires.evidence_text: Pressure is defined mathematically as force per unit area, requiring a prior understanding of force.

requires.label: Mass and Volume
requires.pedagogical_reason: Density is defined as the ratio of mass to volume, requiring prior knowledge of these physical quantities.
requires.evidence_text: Density is defined as the ratio of mass to volume, requiring prior knowledge of these physical quantities.

requires.label: Newton's Third Law of Motion
requires.pedagogical_reason: Used to explain why a fluid at rest cannot exert a force parallel to its surface.
requires.evidence_text: Used to explain why a fluid at rest cannot exert a force parallel to its surface.

support_sections.title: NEWTON’S SECOND LAW OF MOTION
support_sections.summary: This section introduces Newton's second law of motion, defining linear momentum and establishing the quantitative relationship between net external force and acceleration. It explores the vector nature of force, its application to systems of particles, and introduces the concept of impulse.

teaches.label: Impulse
teaches.teaching_evidence: This product is called impulse: Impulse = Force \times time duration = Change in momentum
teaches.evidence_text: This product is called impulse: Impulse = Force \times time duration = Change in momentum

teaches.label: Linear Momentum
teaches.teaching_evidence: Momentum of a body is defined as the product of its mass $m$ and velocity $\mathbf{v}$.
teaches.evidence_text: Momentum of a body is defined as the product of its mass $m$ and velocity $\mathbf{v}$.

teaches.label: Newton (Unit of Force)
teaches.teaching_evidence: One newton is the force that causes an acceleration of $1 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ to a mass of $1 \text{ kg}$.
teaches.evidence_text: One newton is the force that causes an acceleration of $1 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ to a mass of $1 \text{ kg}$.

teaches.label: Newton's Second Law of Motion
teaches.teaching_evidence: The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction in which the force acts.
teaches.evidence_text: The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction in which the force acts.

teaches.label: Vector Components of Force
teaches.teaching_evidence: The vector equation is equivalent to three component equations: $F_x = \frac{dp_x}{dt} = ma_x$, $F_y = \frac{dp_y}{dt} = ma_y$, $F_z = \frac{dp_z}{dt} = ma_z$
teaches.evidence_text: The vector equation is equivalent to three component equations: $F_x = \frac{dp_x}{dt} = ma_x$, $F_y = \frac{dp_y}{dt} = ma_y$, $F_z = \frac{dp_z}{dt} = ma_z$

requires.label: Acceleration
requires.pedagogical_reason: The mathematical derivation of F = ma requires a prior understanding of acceleration as the rate of change of velocity.
requires.evidence_text: The mathematical derivation of F = ma requires a prior understanding of acceleration as the rate of change of velocity.

requires.label: Newton's First Law of Motion
requires.pedagogical_reason: The second law builds directly upon the first law by extending the analysis from zero net external force to non-zero net external force.
requires.evidence_text: The second law builds directly upon the first law by extending the analysis from zero net external force to non-zero net external force.

requires.label: Vector Resolution
requires.pedagogical_reason: Understanding the component equations of Newton's second law requires prior knowledge of resolving vectors into components.
requires.evidence_text: Understanding the component equations of Newton's second law requires prior knowledge of resolving vectors into components.

required_concepts.label: Derivative
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Instantaneous acceleration is defined mathematically as the derivative of velocity with respect to time.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Instantaneous acceleration is defined mathematically as the derivative of velocity with respect to time.

required_concepts.label: Slope of a Line
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Understanding how to find the slope of a line and a tangent is necessary to interpret acceleration from velocity-time graphs.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Understanding how to find the slope of a line and a tangent is necessary to interpret acceleration from velocity-time graphs.

required_concepts.label: Velocity
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity, so the learner must understand velocity and its instantaneous and average forms.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity, so the learner must understand velocity and its instantaneous and average forms.

required_concepts.label: Differentiation
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Calculus-based derivations of velocity and acceleration require differentiating displacement and velocity functions with respect to time.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Calculus-based derivations of velocity and acceleration require differentiating displacement and velocity functions with respect to time.

required_concepts.label: Displacement in Simple Harmonic Motion
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The derivations of velocity and acceleration require the mathematical expression for displacement in SHM as a starting point.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The derivations of velocity and acceleration require the mathematical expression for displacement in SHM as a starting point.

required_concepts.label: Uniform Circular Motion
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The reference circle method relies on projecting the tangential velocity and centripetal acceleration of uniform circular motion onto a diameter.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The reference circle method relies on projecting the tangential velocity and centripetal acceleration of uniform circular motion onto a diameter.

required_concepts.label: Constant Acceleration in One Dimension
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The derivation and understanding of 2D equations of motion build directly upon the 1D equations of motion with constant acceleration.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The derivation and understanding of 2D equations of motion build directly upon the 1D equations of motion with constant acceleration.

required_concepts.label: Vector Components
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Splitting vector equations into independent x and y scalar components requires prior knowledge of vector resolution.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Splitting vector equations into independent x and y scalar components requires prior knowledge of vector resolution.

required_concepts.label: Density
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The concept of uniform density is necessary to calculate the mass of the Earth's inner concentric spheres as a function of radius.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The concept of uniform density is necessary to calculate the mass of the Earth's inner concentric spheres as a function of radius.

required_concepts.label: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The derivations of gravitational forces inside and outside the Earth rely directly on the mathematical formulation of Newton's law of gravitation.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The derivations of gravitational forces inside and outside the Earth rely directly on the mathematical formulation of Newton's law of gravitation.

required_concepts.label: Newton's Second Law of Motion
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Newton's second law (F = ma) is required to relate the gravitational force on a mass to its acceleration due to gravity (g).
required_concepts.evidence_text: Newton's second law (F = ma) is required to relate the gravitational force on a mass to its acceleration due to gravity (g).

required_concepts.label: Binomial Approximation
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Used to simplify the expression for gravity at small heights relative to the Earth's radius.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Used to simplify the expression for gravity at small heights relative to the Earth's radius.

required_concepts.label: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The derivations of gravitational force at a height and depth rely directly on the inverse-square law of gravitation.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The derivations of gravitational force at a height and depth rely directly on the inverse-square law of gravitation.

required_concepts.label: Shell Theorem
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The derivation of gravity at a depth assumes that the outer spherical shell of Earth exerts no net gravitational force on an interior mass.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The derivation of gravity at a depth assumes that the outer spherical shell of Earth exerts no net gravitational force on an interior mass.

required_concepts.label: Instantaneous Acceleration
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Centripetal acceleration is defined as the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Centripetal acceleration is defined as the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero.

required_concepts.label: Position and Velocity Vectors
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The derivation of circular motion kinematics requires representing position and velocity as vectors that change direction over time.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The derivation of circular motion kinematics requires representing position and velocity as vectors that change direction over time.

required_concepts.label: Vector Subtraction
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Finding the direction and magnitude of the change in velocity vector (\Delta \mathbf{v}) relies on vector subtraction and the triangle law of vector addition.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Finding the direction and magnitude of the change in velocity vector (\Delta \mathbf{v}) relies on vector subtraction and the triangle law of vector addition.

required_concepts.label: Acceleration
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The student must understand acceleration and retardation to follow Galileo's observations of motion on inclined planes.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The student must understand acceleration and retardation to follow Galileo's observations of motion on inclined planes.

required_concepts.label: Force
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The concept of inertia is defined in relation to the presence or absence of a net external force.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The concept of inertia is defined in relation to the presence or absence of a net external force.

required_concepts.label: Velocity
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Understanding constant velocity is necessary to comprehend Galileo's conclusions about motion on horizontal planes.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Understanding constant velocity is necessary to comprehend Galileo's conclusions about motion on horizontal planes.

required_concepts.label: Acceleration due to Gravity
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The vertical component of projectile motion is modeled as free fall under constant gravitational acceleration.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The vertical component of projectile motion is modeled as free fall under constant gravitational acceleration.

required_concepts.label: Equations of Motion
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Analyzing projectile motion requires applying kinematic equations of motion independently to the horizontal and vertical directions.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Analyzing projectile motion requires applying kinematic equations of motion independently to the horizontal and vertical directions.

required_concepts.label: Vector Resolution
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The initial velocity vector must be resolved into horizontal and vertical components using trigonometric functions to set up the equations of motion.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The initial velocity vector must be resolved into horizontal and vertical components using trigonometric functions to set up the equations of motion.

required_concepts.label: Meristems
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The section assumes prior knowledge of root apical meristems, shoot apical meristems, and cambium as the source tissues that undergo differentiation.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The section assumes prior knowledge of root apical meristems, shoot apical meristems, and cambium as the source tissues that undergo differentiation.

required_concepts.label: Parenchyma
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The text mentions parenchyma cells as the starting point for dedifferentiation into meristems like interfascicular cambium.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The text mentions parenchyma cells as the starting point for dedifferentiation into meristems like interfascicular cambium.

required_concepts.label: Basic Algebraic Equations
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Required to perform unit conversions, calculate density, and convert temperatures between Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin scales.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Required to perform unit conversions, calculate density, and convert temperatures between Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin scales.

required_concepts.label: Matter
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The student must understand what matter is before studying its physical and chemical properties, mass, volume, and density.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The student must understand what matter is before studying its physical and chemical properties, mass, volume, and density.

required_concepts.label: Force
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Pressure is defined mathematically as force per unit area, requiring a prior understanding of force.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Pressure is defined mathematically as force per unit area, requiring a prior understanding of force.

required_concepts.label: Mass and Volume
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Density is defined as the ratio of mass to volume, requiring prior knowledge of these physical quantities.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Density is defined as the ratio of mass to volume, requiring prior knowledge of these physical quantities.

required_concepts.label: Newton's Third Law of Motion
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Used to explain why a fluid at rest cannot exert a force parallel to its surface.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Used to explain why a fluid at rest cannot exert a force parallel to its surface.

required_concepts.label: Acceleration
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The mathematical derivation of F = ma requires a prior understanding of acceleration as the rate of change of velocity.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The mathematical derivation of F = ma requires a prior understanding of acceleration as the rate of change of velocity.

required_concepts.label: Newton's First Law of Motion
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: The second law builds directly upon the first law by extending the analysis from zero net external force to non-zero net external force.
required_concepts.evidence_text: The second law builds directly upon the first law by extending the analysis from zero net external force to non-zero net external force.

required_concepts.label: Vector Resolution
required_concepts.pedagogical_reason: Understanding the component equations of Newton's second law requires prior knowledge of resolving vectors into components.
required_concepts.evidence_text: Understanding the component equations of Newton's second law requires prior knowledge of resolving vectors into components.

taught_concepts.label: Acceleration
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Acceleration describes the rate at which the velocity of an object changes with time.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Acceleration describes the rate at which the velocity of an object changes with time.

taught_concepts.label: Average Acceleration
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The average acceleration over a time interval is defined as the change of velocity divided by the time interval.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The average acceleration over a time interval is defined as the change of velocity divided by the time interval.

taught_concepts.label: Constant Acceleration
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Although acceleration can vary with time, our study in this chapter will be restricted to motion with constant acceleration.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Although acceleration can vary with time, our study in this chapter will be restricted to motion with constant acceleration.

taught_concepts.label: Displacement from Velocity-Time Graph
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: An interesting feature of a velocity-time graph for any moving object is that the area under the curve represents the displacement over a given time interval.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: An interesting feature of a velocity-time graph for any moving object is that the area under the curve represents the displacement over a given time interval.

taught_concepts.label: Graphical Interpretation of Acceleration
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The acceleration at an instant is the slope of the tangent to the v-t curve at that instant.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The acceleration at an instant is the slope of the tangent to the v-t curve at that instant.

taught_concepts.label: Instantaneous Acceleration
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Instantaneous acceleration is defined in the same way as the instantaneous velocity: a = lim (dv/dt)
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Instantaneous acceleration is defined in the same way as the instantaneous velocity: a = lim (dv/dt)

taught_concepts.label: Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: a(t) = -\omega^2 A \cos (\omega t + \phi) = -\omega^2 x(t)
taught_concepts.evidence_text: a(t) = -\omega^2 A \cos (\omega t + \phi) = -\omega^2 x(t)

taught_concepts.label: Phase Relationships in SHM
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Velocity v(t) has a phase lead of \pi/2 relative to displacement x(t). Acceleration a(t) has a phase lead of \pi relative to displacement x(t)
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Velocity v(t) has a phase lead of \pi/2 relative to displacement x(t). Acceleration a(t) has a phase lead of \pi relative to displacement x(t)

taught_concepts.label: Velocity in Simple Harmonic Motion
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: v(t) = \frac{d}{dt} x(t) = \frac{d}{dt} [A \cos (\omega t + \phi)] = -\omega A \sin (\omega t + \phi)
taught_concepts.evidence_text: v(t) = \frac{d}{dt} x(t) = \frac{d}{dt} [A \cos (\omega t + \phi)] = -\omega A \sin (\omega t + \phi)

taught_concepts.label: Independence of Perpendicular Motions
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: motion in a plane (two dimensions) can be treated as two separate, simultaneous one-dimensional motions with constant acceleration
taught_concepts.evidence_text: motion in a plane (two dimensions) can be treated as two separate, simultaneous one-dimensional motions with constant acceleration

taught_concepts.label: Velocity-Time Relation in a Plane
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: v = v_0 + a t. In component form, this represents two independent equations: v_x = v_{0x} + a_x t and v_y = v_{0y} + a_y t.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: v = v_0 + a t. In component form, this represents two independent equations: v_x = v_{0x} + a_x t and v_y = v_{0y} + a_y t.

taught_concepts.label: Acceleration due to Gravity
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: we obtain the standard formula for acceleration due to gravity at the surface: g = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{GM_E}{R_E^2}.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: we obtain the standard formula for acceleration due to gravity at the surface: g = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{GM_E}{R_E^2}.

taught_concepts.label: Estimation of Earth's Mass
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: enables one to estimate the mass of the earth M_E from the relation M_E = \frac{g R_E^2}{G}.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: enables one to estimate the mass of the earth M_E from the relation M_E = \frac{g R_E^2}{G}.

taught_concepts.label: Gravitational Force Inside the Earth
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: inside the earth, the gravitational force is directly proportional to the distance r from the centre.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: inside the earth, the gravitational force is directly proportional to the distance r from the centre.

taught_concepts.label: Shell Theorem for Gravitation
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: According to the shell theorem, for a point mass outside the Earth, each shell exerts a force as if its mass were concentrated at the center.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: According to the shell theorem, for a point mass outside the Earth, each shell exerts a force as if its mass were concentrated at the center.

taught_concepts.label: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Depth
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The acceleration due to gravity at depth d is g(d) = g(1 - d/R_E).
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The acceleration due to gravity at depth d is g(d) = g(1 - d/R_E).
source_labels: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Depth

taught_concepts.label: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Height
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The acceleration experienced by the point mass is g(h) = GM_E / (R_E + h)^2.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The acceleration experienced by the point mass is g(h) = GM_E / (R_E + h)^2.
source_labels: Acceleration due to Gravity at a Height

taught_concepts.label: Approximation of Gravity for Small Heights
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Using the binomial expression for h/R_E << 1, we get the approximation: g(h) = g(1 - 2h/R_E).
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Using the binomial expression for h/R_E << 1, we get the approximation: g(h) = g(1 - 2h/R_E).
source_labels: Approximation of Gravity for Small Heights

taught_concepts.label: Variation of Gravity with Position
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The acceleration due to earth's gravity is maximum on its surface, decreasing whether you go up... or down.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The acceleration due to earth's gravity is maximum on its surface, decreasing whether you go up... or down.

taught_concepts.label: Angular Displacement and Angular Speed
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: \Delta \theta is called angular displacement. We define the angular speed \omega ... as the time rate of change of angular displacement
taught_concepts.evidence_text: \Delta \theta is called angular displacement. We define the angular speed \omega ... as the time rate of change of angular displacement

taught_concepts.label: Centripetal Acceleration
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: the centripetal acceleration a_c is : a_c = v^2/R
taught_concepts.evidence_text: the centripetal acceleration a_c is : a_c = v^2/R

taught_concepts.label: Relation between Linear Speed and Angular Speed
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Thus, the relation between linear speed v and angular speed \omega is: v = R \omega
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Thus, the relation between linear speed v and angular speed \omega is: v = R \omega
source_labels: Relation between Linear Speed and Angular Speed

taught_concepts.label: Time Period and Frequency in Circular Motion
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The time taken by an object to make one revolution is known as its time period T.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The time taken by an object to make one revolution is known as its time period T.
source_labels: Time Period and Frequency in Circular Motion

taught_concepts.label: Uniform Circular Motion
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: When an object follows a circular path at a constant speed, the motion of the object is called uniform circular motion.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: When an object follows a circular path at a constant speed, the motion of the object is called uniform circular motion.

taught_concepts.label: Galileo's Double Inclined Plane Experiment
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: In the limiting case where the slope of the second plane is zero (horizontal), the ball travels an infinite distance, meaning its motion never ceases
taught_concepts.evidence_text: In the limiting case where the slope of the second plane is zero (horizontal), the ball travels an infinite distance, meaning its motion never ceases

taught_concepts.label: Galileo's Inclined Plane Observations
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: objects moving down an inclined plane accelerate, (ii) objects moving up an inclined plane retard, and (iii) motion on a horizontal plane is an intermediate situation.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: objects moving down an inclined plane accelerate, (ii) objects moving up an inclined plane retard, and (iii) motion on a horizontal plane is an intermediate situation.

taught_concepts.label: Inertia
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The property of a body to resist change in its state of rest or uniform motion is called inertia.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The property of a body to resist change in its state of rest or uniform motion is called inertia.

taught_concepts.label: Horizontal Range of a Projectile
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The horizontal distance travelled by a projectile from its initial position to the position where it passes y=0 during its fall is called the horizontal range, R.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The horizontal distance travelled by a projectile from its initial position to the position where it passes y=0 during its fall is called the horizontal range, R.

taught_concepts.label: Maximum Height of a Projectile
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The maximum height hm reached by the projectile can be calculated by substituting t = tm
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The maximum height hm reached by the projectile can be calculated by substituting t = tm

taught_concepts.label: Projectile Motion
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: A projectile is an object in flight after being thrown or projected... Projectile motion is the result of two independent, simultaneous components
taught_concepts.evidence_text: A projectile is an object in flight after being thrown or projected... Projectile motion is the result of two independent, simultaneous components

taught_concepts.label: Time of Flight
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The total time Tf during which the projectile is in flight... Tf is known as the time of flight of the projectile.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The total time Tf during which the projectile is in flight... Tf is known as the time of flight of the projectile.

taught_concepts.label: Trajectory of a Projectile
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: This is the equation of a parabola, i.e. the path of the projectile is a parabola
taught_concepts.evidence_text: This is the equation of a parabola, i.e. the path of the projectile is a parabola

taught_concepts.label: Dedifferentiation
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: living differentiated cells to regain the capacity to divide under specific conditions, a phenomenon known as dedifferentiation.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: living differentiated cells to regain the capacity to divide under specific conditions, a phenomenon known as dedifferentiation.

taught_concepts.label: Differentiation
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Cells derived from root apical meristems... undergo maturation to perform specific functions, a process called differentiation.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Cells derived from root apical meristems... undergo maturation to perform specific functions, a process called differentiation.

taught_concepts.label: Open Differentiation
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: differentiation in plants is considered 'open' because the final structure of a cell or tissue at maturity is often determined by its location
taught_concepts.evidence_text: differentiation in plants is considered 'open' because the final structure of a cell or tissue at maturity is often determined by its location

taught_concepts.label: Redifferentiation
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: new cells eventually lose their capacity to divide again... this subsequent maturation is called redifferentiation.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: new cells eventually lose their capacity to divide again... this subsequent maturation is called redifferentiation.

taught_concepts.label: Density
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Density is a physical property that relates the mass of a substance to its volume. It is defined as the amount of mass per unit volume
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Density is a physical property that relates the mass of a substance to its volume. It is defined as the amount of mass per unit volume

taught_concepts.label: International System of Units (SI)
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The SI system has seven base units and they are listed in Table 1.1. These units pertain to the seven fundamental scientific quantities.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The SI system has seven base units and they are listed in Table 1.1. These units pertain to the seven fundamental scientific quantities.

taught_concepts.label: Mass and Weight
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Mass of a substance is the amount of matter present in it, while weight is the force exerted by gravity on an object.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Mass of a substance is the amount of matter present in it, while weight is the force exerted by gravity on an object.

taught_concepts.label: Physical and Chemical Properties
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Physical Properties: These can be measured or observed without changing the identity... Chemical Properties: ...requires a chemical change
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Physical Properties: These can be measured or observed without changing the identity... Chemical Properties: ...requires a chemical change

taught_concepts.label: Temperature Scales
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Temperature is commonly measured using three scales: Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin. Kelvin is the SI unit of temperature.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Temperature is commonly measured using three scales: Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin. Kelvin is the SI unit of temperature.

taught_concepts.label: Density
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Density rho for a fluid of mass m occupying volume V is defined as: rho = m/V
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Density rho for a fluid of mass m occupying volume V is defined as: rho = m/V

taught_concepts.label: Normal Force in Fluids
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: When an object is submerged in a fluid at rest, the fluid exerts a force on its surface that is always normal (perpendicular) to the surface
taught_concepts.evidence_text: When an object is submerged in a fluid at rest, the fluid exerts a force on its surface that is always normal (perpendicular) to the surface

taught_concepts.label: Pascal
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: its SI unit is the pascal (Pa), where 1 Pa = 1 N m^-2
taught_concepts.evidence_text: its SI unit is the pascal (Pa), where 1 Pa = 1 N m^-2

taught_concepts.label: Pressure
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: average pressure P_av is defined as the normal force acting per unit area: P_av = F/A
taught_concepts.evidence_text: average pressure P_av is defined as the normal force acting per unit area: P_av = F/A

taught_concepts.label: Relative Density
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Relative density is the dimensionless ratio of a substance's density to the density of water at 4 degrees Celsius
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Relative density is the dimensionless ratio of a substance's density to the density of water at 4 degrees Celsius

taught_concepts.label: Impulse
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: This product is called impulse: Impulse = Force \times time duration = Change in momentum
taught_concepts.evidence_text: This product is called impulse: Impulse = Force \times time duration = Change in momentum

taught_concepts.label: Linear Momentum
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: Momentum of a body is defined as the product of its mass $m$ and velocity $\mathbf{v}$.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: Momentum of a body is defined as the product of its mass $m$ and velocity $\mathbf{v}$.

taught_concepts.label: Newton (Unit of Force)
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: One newton is the force that causes an acceleration of $1 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ to a mass of $1 \text{ kg}$.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: One newton is the force that causes an acceleration of $1 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ to a mass of $1 \text{ kg}$.

taught_concepts.label: Newton's Second Law of Motion
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction in which the force acts.
taught_concepts.evidence_text: The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction in which the force acts.

taught_concepts.label: Vector Components of Force
taught_concepts.teaching_evidence: The vector equation is equivalent to three component equations: $F_x = \frac{dp_x}{dt} = ma_x$, $F_y = \frac{dp_y}{dt} = ma_y$, $F_z = \frac{dp_z}{dt} = ma_z$

ncert:biology:11:13:13.2.title: Differentiation, Dedifferentiation and Redifferentiation
ncert:biology:11:13:13.2.summary: This section explains the developmental processes of differentiation, dedifferentiation, and redifferentiation in plant cells. It describes how cells mature, regain division capacity, and mature again, highlighting that plant differentiation is an open process determined by cell location.

ncert:chemistry:11:1:1.3.title: PROPERTIES OF MATTER AND THEIR MEASUREMENT
ncert:chemistry:11:1:1.3.summary: This section introduces the classification of matter's properties into physical and chemical categories and details the International System of Units (SI) used for quantitative measurements. It defines the seven fundamental base units, SI prefixes, and explains key physical quantities including mass, weight, volume, density, and temperature scales.

ncert:physics:11:13:13.5.title: VELOCITY AND ACCELERATION IN SIMPLE HARMONIC MOTION
ncert:physics:11:13:13.5.summary: This section derives and explains the expressions for velocity and acceleration in simple harmonic motion (SHM) using both the geometric reference circle method and calculus. It also details the phase relationships among displacement, velocity, and acceleration, illustrating them with synchronized plots and numerical calculations.

ncert:physics:11:13:13.6.title: FORCE LAW FOR SIMPLE HARMONIC MOTION
ncert:physics:11:13:13.6.summary: This section derives the force law for simple harmonic motion using Newton's second law, defining the restoring force and force constant. It distinguishes between linear and non-linear oscillators and applies these concepts to analyze a spring-block system.

ncert:physics:11:2:2.3.title: ACCELERATION
ncert:physics:11:2:2.3.summary: This section defines acceleration as the rate of change of velocity with time, distinguishing between average and instantaneous acceleration. It explains how to interpret acceleration graphically using position-time and velocity-time graphs, and demonstrates that the area under a velocity-time graph represents displacement.

ncert:physics:11:3:3.10.title: UNIFORM CIRCULAR MOTION
ncert:physics:11:3:3.10.summary: This section introduces uniform circular motion, defining it as motion along a circular path at a constant speed. It derives the direction and magnitude of centripetal acceleration and establishes the relationships between linear speed, angular speed, time period, and frequency.

ncert:physics:11:3:3.8.title: MOTION IN A PLANE WITH CONSTANT ACCELERATION
ncert:physics:11:3:3.8.summary: This section derives the vector equations of motion for an object moving in a plane with constant acceleration. It establishes that two-dimensional motion can be resolved into two independent, simultaneous one-dimensional motions along perpendicular axes.

ncert:physics:11:3:3.9.title: PROJECTILE MOTION
ncert:physics:11:3:3.9.summary: This section introduces projectile motion as the combination of independent horizontal motion with constant velocity and vertical motion with constant acceleration due to gravity. It derives the parabolic trajectory equation and formulas for key parameters including time of flight, maximum height, and horizontal range.

ncert:physics:11:4:4.3.title: THE LAW OF INERTIA
ncert:physics:11:4:4.3.summary: This section explores Galileo's experiments with inclined and double inclined planes, which challenged the Aristotelian view of motion. These observations led to the definition of inertia as a body's natural resistance to changing its state of rest or uniform motion.

ncert:physics:11:4:4.5.title: NEWTON’S SECOND LAW OF MOTION
ncert:physics:11:4:4.5.summary: This section introduces Newton's second law of motion, defining linear momentum and establishing the quantitative relationship between net external force and acceleration. It explores the vector nature of force, its application to systems of particles, and introduces the concept of impulse.

ncert:physics:11:7:7.5.title: Acceleration Due to Gravity of the Earth
ncert:physics:11:7:7.5.summary: This section explains how to calculate the gravitational force exerted by the Earth on objects located both outside and inside its volume using the shell theorem. It derives the formula for the acceleration due to gravity (g) at the Earth's surface and demonstrates how this value can be used to estimate the Earth's mass.

ncert:physics:11:7:7.6.title: Acceleration Due to Gravity Below and Above the Surface of Earth
ncert:physics:11:7:7.6.summary: This section derives and analyzes the variation of acceleration due to gravity both above and below the Earth's surface. It demonstrates that gravity is at its maximum on the surface and decreases with both altitude and depth.

ncert:physics:11:7:7.8.title: Escape Speed
ncert:physics:11:7:7.8.summary: This section defines and derives the escape speed of an object from a celestial body using the principle of conservation of energy. It calculates the escape speed for Earth and the Moon, discusses its implications for planetary atmospheres, and analyzes projectile motion between two spheres using the concept of a neutral point.

ncert:physics:11:9:9.2.title: Pressure
ncert:physics:11:9:9.2.summary: This section introduces the concept of pressure as the normal force exerted per unit area, explaining its scalar nature, SI units, and behavior in static fluids. It also defines fluid density and relative density, illustrating these concepts with physical examples and data.
